summary: Joel Spolsky announces the shipping of CityDesk, Fog Creek's content management product. He thanks the team, beta testers, families, and blog readers for their support during the development period. He notes the free starter edition can't be downloaded yet because their office T1 line can't handle the expected traffic, and he asks for volunteers to mirror the download. He also highlights a mention in Australian IT news that quoted his philosophy of making products people love.
key_points: CityDesk shipped on time, with a free starter edition and a paid version
Infrastructure limitations (a T1 line) meant they couldn't self-host the free download and needed community help to mirror it
Spolsky's product philosophy centers on usability driving word-of-mouth growth
The post marks a transition from writing about software in the abstract back to reflecting on actually shipping it
money_quotes: CityDesk is shipping! People can buy it! It's a great feeling after a lot of work.
Thanks to you, Joel on Software readers, for bearing with me while I stopped writing about software in the abstract and spent some time doing it.

My number one priority is to make an application that's easy to use so that people like it, so that they tell their friends about it, so that everybody buys it and we have to hire full-time employees just to slit open the envelopes and take out the cheques.
key_insight: Shipping a product is a moment of gratitude and relief, but the real strategy is building something so easy to use that word-of-mouth does the selling.
